mnemonics.title: Fetch-Decode-Execute Steps
mnemonics.content: F-D-E: Fetch (PC→IR, PC+1), Decode (IR→Control, Registers), Execute (ALU/Memory, Write Back)

mnemonics.title: Pipeline Stage Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB: Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Execute, Memory, Write Back - Think 'I Don't Excellent Meals Will Burn' as a memorable phrase for the sequence.

mnemonics.title: Data Hazard Types
mnemonics.content: RAW (Read After Write) - True dependency, WAR (Write After Read) - Anti-dependency, WAW (Write After Write) - Output dependency. Remember: 'RAW is dangerous, WAR is backwards, WAW is output conflict'.

acronyms.usage: Holds address of next instruction to fetch. Incremented after each fetch unless branch/jump changes it.
Holds the currently executing instruction. Contains opcode and operand fields.
Holds the memory address for read/write operations. Connected to address bus.
Temporarily stores data being transferred to/from memory. Acts as buffer.
Performs all arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MULT) and logical (AND, OR, XOR) operations.
Average number of clock cycles to execute one instruction. Ideal pipeline CPI = 1.
True data dependency where instruction must read register after previous instruction writes it.
Anti-dependency where instruction must write register after previous instruction reads it.
Output dependency where two instructions write to same register in order.

visualTips.visualization: Draw as horizontal rows for instructions (I1, I2, I3, I4) and vertical columns for clock cycles. Show diagonal pattern where each instruction moves right one stage each cycle. After cycle 5, one instruction completes every cycle - this diagonal throughput visual demonstrates pipeline parallelism.

Think of datapath as a factory assembly line: Registers (storage) → Bus (conveyor) → ALU (workstation) → Bus → Registers. Control signals are the supervisor instructions at each station. Draw boxes for PC, MAR, MDR, IR, Registers, ALU with arrows showing data flow direction.

Use a timeline diagram showing instruction execution in pipeline stages. For RAW hazard, draw the writing instruction completing MEM/WB before the reading instruction enters EX. For control hazard, show the 'bubble' (empty pipeline slot) created when branch outcome is unknown.
Speedup = Pipeline Depth / (1 + (Pipeline Depth - 1)/n). Draw hyperbola approaching k as n increases. Shows why deeper pipelines have diminishing returns for short programs.

analogies.analogy: Like a car assembly line with 5 stations: each station does one specialized task (engine, wheels, body, paint, inspection). Once pipeline is full, one car completes every minute regardless of total stations - throughput vs latency trade-off.

Two people passing a basketball: Person A must throw before Person B can catch. If Person B tries to catch before Person A throws (pipeline without hazard handling), they get air - the data isn't ready yet.
Following a recipe but not knowing the next step until you've completed current step - you must wait (stall) or guess (predict). Misprediction means starting over (pipeline flush).

A register file is like a team's shared whiteboard - multiple people can read it simultaneously (2 read ports), but only one should write at a time to avoid confusion. Zero access time because it's right there, not in memory (like a library down the hall).
